content.text: Scheduled Upgrade Windows and Rollback Plans

Ad-hoc dependency upgrades — applied whenever a developer has time — produce an unpredictable production environment where it is impossible to attribute behavioral changes to specific updates. Scheduled upgrade windows impose order: upgrades happen at known times, go through a defined procedure, and are followed by a structured observation period. Rollback plans define in advance exactly when and how to revert, so the decision is not made under pressure when something goes wrong.

attrs.alt: Weekly upgrade window structure Monday through Friday, with rollback decision tree based on error rate, safety bypasses, and latency regression

content.text: The upgrade window is a recurring time slot during which planned dependency upgrades are deployed to production. It has three design parameters: timing, duration, and freeze period.
The ideal window is mid-week, mid-morning UTC — typically Tuesday or Wednesday between 09:00 and 15:00 UTC. The reasoning:

: Monday is high-risk (teams are ramping up, alerting may be misconfigured after a weekend). Friday is high-risk (on-call coverage drops over weekends; a Friday deploy gone wrong has a 60-hour blast radius before Monday engineering coverage resumes).
: Covers both EU and US East Coast business hours, ensuring engineering coverage during the observation period. Avoid times when only one region is awake.
: Customer usage spikes at period close for many B2B applications; this is not the time to introduce a deployment risk.

For global applications with follow-the-sun engineering coverage, two windows are sometimes needed — one in the EU morning and one in the US morning. Each window deploys to a different region first, using the first region as a longer canary for the second.
Each upgrade window handles a batch of dependencies from the priority queue. Batch sizing is constrained by the time available for the observation period within the window.
Do not batch a Critical CVE with other dependencies in the same window.
If the Critical patch causes a rollback, you will not know which package in the batch was responsible. Single-package deploys for Critical CVEs are non-negotiable.
The freeze period is when no planned upgrades are deployed. The goal is to ensure that on-call engineers are not simultaneously managing a fresh deploy and reduced weekend coverage.
The 5 business days bracketing major holidays (e.g., the week of December 24 through January 1 for US/EU customers)
Critical CVE patches override any freeze with incident commander approval. This exception must be documented and reviewed in the next post-mortem.

The window follows a defined sequence. Deviation from this sequence — skipping the Monday pre-check, deploying directly to production without staging, skipping the observation period — increases rollback probability and decreases the team's ability to diagnose what went wrong.
The day before the window, the engineer assigned to the upgrade performs:
from the inventory system. Confirm which packages will be upgraded in tomorrow's window.
for each package being upgraded. Look for breaking changes, deprecations, and behavior changes. For LLM SDKs, check the migration guide explicitly.
on a branch and confirm that all unit and integration tests pass:  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'pytest tests/ -v', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]} .
if the batch includes LLM SDK or inference runtime upgrades. The baseline captured Monday becomes the comparison target for Tuesday's post-deploy check.
for the 24-hour observation period. If the on-call engineer cannot cover Wednesday, reschedule the window.

#!/bin/bash
# upgrade_window.sh — execute the upgrade window procedure

set -e  # exit on any error

PACKAGES=("openai==1.31.0" "httpx==0.27.0")  # from priority queue
BRANCH="upgrade/2025-07-01-weekly"

echo "=== UPGRADE WINDOW: $(date -u) ==="

# 1. Create upgrade branch
git checkout main && git pull origin main
git checkout -b "$BRANCH"

# 2. Apply version bumps
pip install "${PACKAGES[@]}"
pip freeze | grep -E "$(IFS="|"; echo "${PACKAGES[*]%%=*}")" >> requirements.txt
# Lock file update
pip-compile requirements.in --output-file requirements.lock

# 3. Run targeted tests
echo "--- Running unit + integration tests ---"
pytest tests/ -v --tb=short

# 4. Build image and deploy to staging
echo "--- Deploying to staging ---"
docker build -t genai-app:upgrade-candidate .
kubectl set image deployment/genai-api genai-api=genai-app:upgrade-candidate -n staging
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-api -n staging

# 5. Run golden prompt comparison (if applicable)
if [[ " ${PACKAGES[*]} " =~ "openai" ]]; then
    echo "--- Running golden prompt comparison ---"
    python3 scripts/golden_prompt_runner.py --output golden_after.json
    python3 scripts/compare_golden.py golden_before.json golden_after.json
fi

# 6. Canary deploy to production (10%)
echo "--- Canary deploy to production (10%) ---"
kubectl set image deployment/genai-api genai-api=genai-app:upgrade-candidate -n production
kubectl scale deployment/genai-api --replicas=1 -n production  # canary: 1 of 10 pods

echo "=== Canary deployed. Observe for 60 minutes before promoting. ==="
The canary observation window is where the rollback decision tree runs. The on-call engineer monitors four signals during the observation period:

An alert on any of these signals triggers the rollback decision tree. The decision tree asks three sequential questions: (1) is the error rate elevated? (2) is there a safety bypass? (3) is latency regressed? A yes on any question triggers the defined action — rollback for errors and safety bypasses, investigate for latency.
After 24 hours of clean canary signals, promote the canary to full production rollout:

#!/bin/bash
# promote.sh — promote canary to full production after 24h observation

set -e

echo "=== PROMOTING canary to full rollout: $(date -u) ==="

# Scale all pods to new image
kubectl rollout restart deployment/genai-api -n production

# Wait for rollout to complete
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-api -n production --timeout=10m

# Verify
CURRENT_IMAGE=$(kubectl get deployment/genai-api -n production     -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}')
echo "Production image: $CURRENT_IMAGE"

# Update SBOM (Software Bill of Materials)
python3 scripts/update_sbom.py --deployment production

# Close the upgrade ticket
# (Integrate with your issue tracker)

echo "=== Promotion complete. Update changelog and close upgrade ticket. ==="

If the 24-hour observation surfaced a rollback trigger, execute the rollback instead. The rollback procedure is pre-staged — the previous image tag is known and the rollback command is documented in the upgrade ticket before deployment begins.
A rollback plan is not the rollback command itself — it is the set of decisions made before deployment that make rollback fast and unambiguous during an incident.
Before every upgrade window deployment, the engineer records four items in the upgrade ticket:
the exact container image running in production before the upgrade
copy-pasteable, tested in staging, specific to this deployment
the specific observable conditions that will initiate a rollback — not 'if things look bad' but 'if error rate increases more than 1% within 15 minutes of canary deploy'
the named on-call engineer authorized to execute the rollback without additional approval during the observation period

# Upgrade ticket rollback section (template)
# Filled out BEFORE the upgrade window begins

rollback_plan:
  pre_upgrade_image: "genai-app:sha-abc12345"
  rollback_command: |
    kubectl set image deployment/genai-api       genai-api=genai-app:sha-abc12345 -n production
    kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-api -n production
  rollback_triggers:
    - "HTTP 5xx rate increases more than 1% above 7-day baseline"
    - "Any safety regression: a previously-blocked prompt now passes through"
    - "P99 inference latency exceeds 10% above baseline for 15+ consecutive minutes"
    - "More than 3 customer-reported 'unexpected output' tickets in 60 minutes"
  rollback_owner: "@on-call-engineer"
  rollback_decision_authority: "On-call engineer — no IC approval needed for automated trigger hits"
  estimated_rollback_time: "5 minutes to image swap, 10 minutes to full rollout"
Not all rollbacks are equally clean. Three dependency categories have rollback complications that must be planned for before the upgrade:
LLM SDK rollbacks and cached sessions

: If the SDK version change affected the request format sent to the LLM provider, in-flight sessions may have mixed-version state. Flush the session cache after rollback to prevent users from hitting cached responses built under the new format.

: If the dependency upgrade included a database schema migration, a rollback to the previous application version may be incompatible with the migrated schema. The rollback plan must include either a down-migration script or a decision that the schema change is forward-compatible.

: If the upgrade changed the embedding model used to generate vector store entries, the existing embeddings in the vector store are from the old model. After rollback, the application will query the vector store with the old model's embedding format, which is consistent. But documents embedded under the new model version during the window need to be re-embedded under the old model or the vector store needs to be rebuilt. This is the most expensive rollback type — plan a maintenance window for it explicitly.
After executing a rollback, confirm the system is back to baseline before declaring the rollback complete:

#!/bin/bash
# validate_rollback.sh — confirm production is back to pre-upgrade state

set -e

EXPECTED_IMAGE="genai-app:sha-abc12345"  # from rollback plan

# Confirm image
CURRENT=$(kubectl get deployment/genai-api -n production     -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}')
if [[ "$CURRENT" != "$EXPECTED_IMAGE" ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: current image $CURRENT != expected $EXPECTED_IMAGE"
    exit 1
fi
echo "Image confirmed: $CURRENT"

# Confirm rollout is complete
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-api -n production

# Run smoke tests against production
pytest tests/integration/test_smoke.py     --base-url=https://api.example.com     -v --tb=short

# Check error rate via CLI (if Datadog is configured)
# datadog-ci metric query "avg:genai.http.5xx_rate{env:production}" --last 5m

echo "=== Rollback validated. Open post-mortem ticket. ==="

Model weight upgrades — switching from one underlying model version to another — are a distinct category from dependency package upgrades. They require a dedicated upgrade window with a more extensive evaluation suite because the behavioral surface area is larger.
— run the new model version in parallel with the current model on 5% of production traffic. Log both responses. The ML engineer reviews sampled response pairs for quality differences.
— run the 200-prompt golden prompt set, the safety regression suite, and the performance baseline against the new model.
— route 10% of production traffic to the new model. Monitor CSAT, safety event rate, and latency for 48 hours (longer than standard dependency canary).
— if the canary shows no regressions, expand to 50% for another 24 hours.
— if stable at 50%, promote to 100% and retire the old model endpoint.

The 48-hour canary for model weight upgrades (vs. 24 hours for dependency upgrades) exists because model output quality issues often surface in low-frequency input patterns that require a longer observation window to accumulate enough traffic.
Upgrade windows generate data that drives continuous improvement of the upgrade process. Three metrics are worth tracking per window and reviewing monthly:

What percentage of scheduled upgrade windows completed successfully (no rollback, no postponement)? A completion rate below 80% indicates that either the pre-check process is missing problems that surface during deployment, or the rollback triggers are too sensitive.
— the most valuable learning. Each rollback should produce a post-mortem that answers: was the problem detectable during the Monday pre-check? What would have caught it?
— if windows are regularly postponed because on-call engineers are not available, the window scheduling process is broken
— a Critical CVE that forced an emergency deploy into a freeze period. Count these separately; they indicate emergency patch volume, not window scheduling problems
MTFR measures the elapsed time from 'canary deployed' to 'full production rollout complete'. This is the actual blast radius window for any problem that was not caught in staging.
24h window less buffer; prioritize speed over extended observation
Same standard; batch size does not change observation time
Longer observation for quality drift detection

MTFR above target indicates that the observation period is being extended due to inconclusive signals — a sign that monitoring coverage is insufficient to make a confident promote/rollback decision within the planned window.

Tracking rollback rate by category (LLM SDK, inference runtime, safety guardrail, etc.) reveals which dependency types are consistently problematic and need more thorough pre-checks or staging evaluation before production deployment.

Upgrade windows must be registered in your organization's change management system, especially for customers with compliance requirements. A GenAI application serving financial services or healthcare customers is likely subject to change control requirements that mandate documentation of every production change.
Minimum fields for a change record covering a weekly upgrade window:
Standard (pre-approved for routine dependency upgrades); Emergency for CVE break-glass
List with old version and new version for each package
Priority tier from inventory scoring, CVE IDs if applicable
Links to CI run showing unit/integration/golden prompt tests passed
Link to rollback plan section in upgrade ticket
Expected: none. If output behavior changes are possible, note which features are affected
For High/Critical: confirm security advisory distributed
Communicating Upgrade Windows to Enterprise Customers

Enterprise customers — particularly those with operational approval processes of their own — appreciate advance notice of planned upgrade windows. A monthly upgrade schedule published at the start of each month sets expectations and reduces inbound 'what changed on Tuesday?' support tickets.

Planned Maintenance Schedule — July 2025
[Company Name] GenAI Platform

Standard upgrade windows: Every Tuesday, 10:00–14:00 UTC
Freeze period: Fridays after 14:00 UTC through Sunday 23:59 UTC

Week of July 1:
  No planned upgrades.

Week of July 8:
  Planned: LLM SDK minor version update (openai 1.30 -> 1.31)
  Risk: Low — no breaking changes in changelog.
  Expected impact: None.

Week of July 15:
  Planned: Inference runtime security patch (addresses CVE-2025-4421)
  Risk: Medium — security patch; tested against golden prompt set.
  Expected impact: None expected. Canary observation 24h.

Week of July 22:
  Planned: Routine dependency batch (12 low/medium packages)
  Risk: Low.
  Expected impact: None.

Questions? Contact your CSM or reach us at platform-ops@[yourdomain].com.

Scheduled upgrade windows impose predictability on what would otherwise be an unpredictable production environment. The Tuesday window structure — Monday pre-check, Tuesday deploy to staging and canary, Wednesday observation, Thursday promotion or rollback — matches the cadence to the risk of the change. Critical CVEs break the schedule; everything else follows it.

Rollback plans are the defensive counterpart to the upgrade window. By defining rollback triggers, commands, and owners before deployment begins, the team transforms a high-pressure incident decision into a mechanical execution of a pre-made choice. The observation period — 24 hours for dependency upgrades, 48–72 hours for model weight upgrades — is the window in which automated monitoring enforces the rollback decision tree without requiring human judgment.
